features.popup:
Kejimkoojik Lake - Fairy Rocks

Nova Scotia, Canada · Incised/scratched

Location: East side of Kejimkoojik Lake, south of entrance to Fairy Lake, Queens County; boundary of Annapolis and Queens counties on Liverpool River

350+ engravings and groups including human figures, animals, chieftainess with headdress, mantis, moose; three palimpsest horizons (Mi'kmaq oldest, French middle ~1700, English most recent post-1800)

Rock: Schistose slate, Silurian formation. Setting: Central rock: irregular oval 47x60 ft exposed; highest points 2-3 ft above water. Attribution: Mi'kmaq (Micmac).

Condition: Many inscriptions submerged; palimpsest with 2-3 layers; modern graffiti (Sunday school excursion); earliest markings nearly invisible without careful inspection

Water connection: Lake - rocks partially submerged, water level varies; on ancient Indian trail

Acadian refugees halted here during 1756 expulsion; earlier French presence after Port Royal capture 1710; Louis Labrador family tradition

Sources: Mallery fieldwork 1887-1888; George Creed copies; Mallery 1893: pp. 37-42 (Figs. 1, 2, 549, 550, 654, 655, 656, 657, 658, 717, 718, 739, 740, 741, 1254, 1255, 1262)

Kejimkoojik Lake - Piels Point

Nova Scotia, Canada · Incised/scratched

Location: About 2 miles south of Fairy Rocks, at Piels (Pierre's) Point, opposite Glodes/Gload Island, Kejimkoojik Lake

Same character as Fairy Rocks engravings; continuation of same formation

Rock: Schistose slate, Silurian formation. Setting: Steeper inclination than Fairy Rocks. Attribution: Mi'kmaq (Micmac).

Condition: Part of same complex as Fairy Rocks

Water connection: Lake shore

Gload Island named from well-known Micmac family

Sources: Mallery fieldwork; Mallery 1893: pp. 38

Fort Medway River Petroglyphs

Nova Scotia, Canada · Incised

Location: Fort Medway River, vicinity of Kejimkoojik Lake, Queens County

Same character as Kejimkoojik Lake engravings

Rock: Schistose slate. Attribution: Mi'kmaq (Micmac).

Water connection: River

Sources: Mallery fieldwork; Mallery 1893: pp. 38

Georges Lake Petroglyphs

Nova Scotia, Canada · Incised

Location: Georges Lake, vicinity of Kejimkoojik Lake, Queens County

Same character as Kejimkoojik Lake engravings

Rock: Schistose slate. Attribution: Mi'kmaq (Micmac).

Water connection: Lake

Sources: Mallery fieldwork; Mallery 1893: pp. 38

Nipigon Bay Pictographs

Ontario, Canada · Painted (blood-red pigment)

Location: Nipigon Bay, 30 miles NW of Lake Superior, connected by Nipigon River

Men, animals, Indians in canoes, wild animals; zone 60 ft long, 5 ft broad on face of rock; pictures 10-20 ft above water

Rock: Red rock cliff. Setting: Rock face ~60 ft fronting water, ~900 yards wide. Attribution: Unknown; local Indians have no traditions about them.

Condition: Paint indelible by unknown process

Water connection: Lake bay - rock fronts water

No living traditions; 'supposed to have been painted ages ago'

Sources: Charles Hallock; Newton Flanagan (Hudson Bay Company); Mallery 1893: pp. 42-43

Roche Percee

Manitoba, Canada · Pictographs

Location: On Souris River, near international boundary, 270 miles west of Dufferin, nearly due north from Bismarck

Covered with pictographs of memorable events

Setting: Isolated rock in middle of a plain.

Water connection: Half mile from Souris River

Sources: Charles Hallock; Mallery 1893: pp. 43

Lake of the Woods Peninsula Petroglyphs

Manitoba/Keewatin, Canada · Carved/deeply cut

Location: North side of large peninsula extending into Lake of the Woods; south shore of northern half of lake, midway between east and west shores

Spirals, concentric circles, crosses, horseshoe forms, arrow shapes; similar to SW United States and Brazilian petroglyphs

Rock: Soft foliated green chloritic schist (roche moutonnee). Setting: Low glaciated projecting point, few feet above high-water mark. Attribution: Unknown; attributed to 'old people' long ago.

Condition: Weathering comparable to glacial striations

Water connection: Lake shore; water rises/falls through 10 ft range

Indians say made by 'old people' long ago; weathering comparable to glacial grooves

Sources: A.C. Lawson; Mallery 1893: pp. 43-44

Crow-Rock Island (Ka-ka-ki-wa-bic min-nis)

Manitoba/Keewatin, Canada · Painted (ochre)

Location: Island in Lake of the Woods, less than a mile west of peninsula site; south side of island

Ochre-painted characters on overhanging wall; similar to peninsula petroglyphs

Rock: Hard greenstone. Setting: Steep rocky island; overhanging wall surface. Attribution: Unknown.

Condition: Much faded in places; part of overhanging wall has fallen down

Water connection: Island in lake

Sources: A.C. Lawson; Mallery 1893: pp. 43-44

Sproat Lake Petroglyph

British Columbia, Canada · Scraped grooves (sticks rubbing moist sand); 2-3 fingers bre

Location: Eastern shore of Sproat Lake, near southern outlet; ~10 km north of upper end of Alberni Fiord, Vancouver Island

Fishes or marine monsters; possibly a manned boat; Nootka-style designs

Rock: Perpendicular rock wall. Setting: Rock wall ~7 meters high dropping directly into lake. Attribution: Hopetschisath (Nootka/Aht); territory also of Ts'eschaath and Kowitchin.

Condition: Much weathered, hardly recognizable in places; broad cleft in rock with fallen blocks that bore part of drawing

Water connection: Lake - rock drops directly into water; copy made while standing in water

Rock was once house of Kwotiath, wandering divinity in Nootka mythology (corresponds to Raven of Tlingit/Haida, Qals of Kowitchin)

Sources: Franz Boas; G.M. Sproat; Mallery 1893: pp. 44-45 (Figs. 3)

Fort Wrangell / Stikine Village Petroglyphs

Alaska · Carved; also painted (black, light green, dark red)

Location: Rocks from ancient village of Stikine, near Fort Wrangell; also on rocks above high-water mark around ruined and abandoned villages

Human faces in Alaskan typical style; orca/whale killer (demon Skana); conventional animal signs (bear=protruding tongue, beaver/wolf=teeth, orca=fin, raven=sharp beak, eagle=curved beak)

Attribution: Haida.

Water connection: Coastal; above high-water mark

Orca believed to be demon called Skana with many mythic tales

Sources: Albert P. Niblack, U.S. Navy; Mallery 1893: pp. 47 (Figs. 4)

San Francisco Mountain Area Petroglyphs

Arizona · Pecked (breaking through weathered film); one exception pain

Location: About 35 miles east and southeast of San Francisco Mountain

Shoshonean type; mealing stones, pottery fragments, chipped flints nearby indicating sedentary Indians; many localities

Rock: Red sandstone, low buttes on plain. Attribution: Shoshonean type.

Sources: G.K. Gilbert, U.S. Geological Survey; Mallery 1893: pp. 48-49 (Figs. 5, 6, 7)

Gila River - Powers Butte Pictographs

Arizona · Pictographs

Location: 8 miles below Powers Butte, on mesa bordering Gila River, rising 150 ft; also summit of spur of Oatman Mountain, 200 yards from Gila, 300 ft above it

Covering entire vertical face of mesa; numbers of pictographs on Oatman Mountain spur

Setting: Mesa rises 150 ft; Oatman spur 300 ft above river.

Condition: Many almost obliterated on exposed surfaces

Water connection: River - bordering Gila River

Sources: Paul Holman, U.S. Geological Survey; Mallery 1893: pp. 49

Gila Bend Granite Mound

Arizona · Unknown characters; some modern, some ancient

Location: Table-land near Gila Bend; also at 32 38 13 N, 109 7 30 W on Gila River

Unknown characters on blackened bowlders; figures on bowlders and cliff face to height of 30 ft

Rock: Granite bowlders blackened by augite. Attribution: Some modern Indian, some of 'undoubted antiquity'.

Condition: Mix of modern and ancient

Water connection: River - on Gila River

Sources: Lt. Col. Emory; Mallery 1893: pp. 49

Yampais Spring Petroglyphs

Arizona · Pictographs (painted)

Location: Yampais Spring, Williams River; secluded glen among mountains with cave, pool and crystal stream

Lower surface of rock covered with pictographs; none appear recent

Rock: Shelving rock forming cave.

Condition: Not recent

Water connection: Spring/stream in cave

Sources: Lt. Whipple; Mallery 1893: pp. 50

Colorado Plateau East of Peach Springs

Arizona · Pecked with harder stone

Location: Colorado Plateau of northern Arizona, east of Peach Springs

Sun, human beings in grotesque styles, other characters; considerable artistic workmanship

Rock: Basalt.

Sources: W.J. Hoffman (1872); Mallery 1893: pp. 50

Partridge Creek Petroglyphs

Arizona · Pecked with sharp point (obsidian abundant nearby)

Location: Partridge Creek, northern Arizona, where Beale wagon road comes to it from the east

Resemble Moki Pueblo characters; some fresh indicating locality still in use

Rock: Cross-laminated Aubrey sandstone. Attribution: Resemble Moki Pueblo; hunting ground of Wallapai and Havasupai (Cosninos).

Condition: Some inscriptions very fresh; locality still resorted to

Water connection: Creek

Sources: G.K. Gilbert (November 1878); Mallery 1893: pp. 50 (Figs. 8)

Rio Verde Valley Petroglyphs

Arizona · Rock drawings

Location: Valley of Rio Verde, from below Camp Verde to the Gila River

Frequent occurrence along entire valley extent

Water connection: River valley

Sources: Mallery general description; Mallery 1893: pp. 50

Canyon Segy and Keam's Canyon

Arizona · Rock drawings

Location: Canyon Segy and Keam's Canyon, northeastern Arizona

Some forms also found on Moki pottery

Attribution: Moki (Hopi) connection.

Water connection: Canyon

Sources: Thomas V. Keam; Mallery 1893: pp. 50

Chinlee Creek Petroglyphs

Arizona · Pecked

Location: Chinlee (Chilali) Creek, 30-40 miles from confluence with San Juan River

Shoshonean type; resembling Owens Valley California petroglyphs

Attribution: Shoshonean type.

Water connection: Creek

Sources: Lt. Theodore Mosher; Lt. Casey's party (December 1887); Mallery 1893: pp. 51

Gila River Valley Sculptured Bowlders

Arizona · Pecked with sharp instrument

Location: Valley of the Gila River

Hundreds of bowlders with rude figures of men, animals, grotesque forms; concentric circles with lines; some sculptures on undersides of bowlders (multi-ton rocks)

Rock: Bowlders. Setting: Some bowlders weigh many tons.

Condition: Many defaced by weathering and subsequent markings; few seem recent, others bear marks of great antiquity

Water connection: River valley

Sources: J.R. Bartlett; Mallery 1893: pp. 51 (Figs. 8)

Shinumo Canyon Petroglyphs

Arizona · Painted (red, white, yellow)

Location: Shinumo Canyon

Human form with white mark on abdomen (middle and right figures red); left-hand man painted yellow with two red plumes

Water connection: Canyon

Sources: F.S. Dellenbaugh sketches; Mallery 1893: pp. 51 (Figs. 9)

Mound Canyon Petroglyphs

Arizona · Petroglyphs (rather indistinct)

Location: Mound Canyon, vertical wall

Most conspicuous forms appear to be serpents

Condition: Rather indistinct

Water connection: Canyon

Sources: F.S. Dellenbaugh sketches; Mallery 1893: pp. 51 (Figs. 10)

Rocky Hill / Rocky Point Petroglyphs

California · Painted (red, white, black pigments)

Location: Rocky Hill (Rocky Point), ~12-15 miles east of Visalia, Tulare County; south of Yokohl Creek, in first foothills

Men, animals, deer, antelope, coyotes, birds, turtles, circles, spirals, crowns, bars; vivid where protected

Rock: Huge granite cropping (split, leaving plane surface). Attribution: Possibly totemic characters or hunting records.

Condition: Drawn many years ago; many now indistinct; granite being quarried at site

Water connection: Foothills; near creek

Sources: H.W. Turner (June 1891); Mallery 1893: pp. 52 (Figs. 11)

Tule River Agency Rock Painting

California · Pecked then painted (red ocher, yellow ocherous clay, white

Location: Tule River Agency, western side of Sierra Nevada, headwater canyons of south fork of Tule River, Kern County

Largest human figure 6 ft tall; beaver, bear, centipede, bald eagle on ceiling; insect forms with exaggerated antennae; blending of bear and human forms (Shoshonean type); 12-15 ft wide, 8 ft high painted surface

Rock: Immense granite bowlder ~20 ft thick, 30 ft long; with square passageway through it. Setting: Painted surface 12-15 ft wide, 8 ft high; largest figure 6 ft; bowlder 20x30 ft. Attribution: Mariposan linguistic stock territory; ancestors of present tribes found it already there.

Condition: ~100 years old based on weathering; color penetrated rock interstices; varnish-like coating on pigment mortars

Water connection: Canyon headwaters

Tribes say records were found by their ancestors; pigment mixing mortars found (2 inch diameter, 1 inch deep depressions with varnish-like coating)

Sources: W.J. Hoffman (summer 1882); Mallery 1893: pp. 52-56 (Figs. 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 983)

Owens Valley - Watterson's Ranch Petroglyphs

California · Deeply pecked (1/2 to 1.5 inches deep); footprints finished

Location: Northern Owens Valley, between White Mountains (east) and Benton Range (west); detached low butte/mesa at Watterson's Ranch, western slope of Benton Range

Footprints (cup-like toes, 15 inches long), circles, bear tracks, human forms, serpents (including double-headed), Shoshonean type; oxidized surfaces match original rock patina suggesting considerable antiquity

Rock: Blackened basaltic bowlders and cliffs. Setting: Footprints average 15 inches length, depth 1/2 to 1+ inch. Attribution: Shoshonean type; Pai-Ute currently in area but unacquainted with significance; obsidian camp remains nearby may be from same people.

Condition: Sculpted surfaces oxidized to same shining black as original surface; indicating considerable antiquity

Water connection: Abundance of springs; stream running toward Black Lake

Pai-Ute declare no knowledge of authors; old obsidian camp with bleached/friable specimens suggesting great age

Sources: W.J. Hoffman (1871, autumn 1884); Mallery 1893: pp. 57-58 (Figs. Plates I-VII)

Owens Valley - Chalk Grade Petroglyphs

California · Pecked (1/4 to 1/2 inch deep)

Location: Chalk Grade, southernmost extremity of Owens Valley petroglyph series; east side of old stage road through small valley, ~6 miles south of northernmost group

Elaborate cross (surface pecked out to leave cross standing in relief); animal forms including antelope; circles with interior decoration (pairs of lines, zigzag, cross lines); cervus/antelocapra footprints

Rock: Immense basalt bowlders. Attribution: Shoshonean type.

Condition: Some defaced by sand erosion from strong summer winds; freshly broken surfaces oxidize to brownish-black; arid desolate country

Water connection: Arid; no water nearby

Sources: W.J. Hoffman (autumn 1884); Mallery 1893: pp. 59-60 (Figs. 17, Plates VIII-XI)

Death Valley - Saratoga Springs Petroglyphs

California · Petroglyphs

Location: About 200 yards from Saratoga Springs, on side of a hill, lower end of Death Valley

Multiple groups; extensive ancient camping ground nearby with obsidian, chert, chalcedony, flint chips from arrowpoint manufacture

Attribution: Shoshoni inhabit region; few families near Panamint Mountains.

Condition: Some too indistinct to copy due to age/weathering; smaller groups and single figures on nearby rocks

Water connection: Near springs with ponds and tule marsh (60-80 acre basin)

Sources: E.W. Nelson (February 1891); Mallery 1893: pp. 60-61 (Figs. 18)

Emigrant Canyon - Panamint Mountains

California · Petroglyphs

Location: Emigrant Canyon, Panamint Mountains (near Death Valley)

Large number in groups; Shoshonean type including 'Moki goats', tridents, Greek psi, many crosses

Rock: Ledge. Attribution: Shoshonean type.

Condition: Photograph received too late for insertion in Mallery's work

Water connection: Canyon

Sources: C. Hart Merriam, Dept. of Agriculture; Mallery 1893: pp. 61

Rattlesnake Rock - Mojave Desert

California · Pecked with dull-pointed stone (lines not sharp, show striat

Location: About 2 miles north of Daggett Station, Mojave Desert

Characters on rock; named for abundant rattlesnakes sheltering in rock mass

Rock: Small porphyritic butte.

Water connection: Desert

Sources: Mining and Scientific Press; Mallery 1893: pp. 61 (Figs. 19)

Piute Creek Petroglyphs

California · Carved

Location: Piute Creek, about 30 miles west of Mojave villages

Numerous; resemble drawings in NE Arizona, southern Utah, western New Mexico

Condition: Appear old; confusedly obscured

Water connection: Creek

Sources: Lt. Whipple; Mallery 1893: pp. 62

San Marcos Pass Petroglyphs

California · Painted (red and black)

Location: 15 miles west of Santa Barbara, northern summit of Santa Ynez Range, near San Marcos Pass; cavity near base of immense bowlder over 20 ft high

Checkerboard pattern squares, serpentine/zigzag lines, sun figures, short parallel line groups, insect forms, serratures on curved lines

Rock: Immense bowlder (20+ ft); nearby flat granitic bowlder with 21 mortar holes. Setting: Bowlder over 20 ft high. Attribution: Indians visiting during acorn season (oaks abundant).

21 mortar holes for grinding acorns nearby; on one of four old Indian trails crossing mountains

Sources: W.J. Hoffman; Mallery 1893: pp. 64-65 (Figs. 20, 21, 22)

Najowe Valley Petroglyphs

California · Painted (black=manganese compound, red=ferruginous clays)

Location: Najowe Valley, 43 miles west of Santa Barbara; promontory base with large shallow cavern

Human figures with gesture poses (surprise/astonishment, negation); insect forms; horned cattle (later addition); forms similar to San Marcos Pass and Tulare/Owens Valley

Rock: Rock cavern (opening smaller than interior).

Condition: Some characters drawn at later date (horned cattle)

Sources: W.J. Hoffman; Mallery 1893: pp. 65-68 (Figs. 23, 24, 25, 26, 27, 28)

Carisa Plain Petroglyphs (Orena Ranch)

California · Painted

Location: Carisa Plain, near Mr. Orena's ranch, 60-70 miles due north of Santa Barbara

Most conspicuous: sun figure resembling human face with ornamental appendages at cardinal points (resembles Moki masks); serpentine lines, anomalous forms; most extensive and elaborately drawn grouping

Attribution: Resembles Moki style.

Sources: W.J. Hoffman; Mallery 1893: pp. 68

Stevens Residence Petroglyph (Santa Barbara)

California · Painted/engraved

Location: 4 miles NE of Santa Barbara, near residence of Mr. Stevens

Human figures in gesture for negation; designs on western side in slight cavity

Rock: Isolated sandstone bowlder, 20 ft high, 30 ft diameter. Setting: Bowlder 20 ft high, 30 ft diameter.

Sources: W.J. Hoffman; Mallery 1893: pp. 68 (Figs. 29)

Pulpit Rock (Dr. Coe's Farm)

California · Engraved

Location: Half mile east of Stevens residence, on Dr. Coe's farm, near Santa Barbara

Various engravings in cavity; named 'Pulpit Rock' for shape and echo properties

Rock: Smaller bowlder.

Condition: Parts disappeared through rock disintegration

Water connection: Narrow valley

Named for echo observed when speaking above ordinary tone

Sources: W.J. Hoffman; Mallery 1893: pp. 68 (Figs. 30)

Azuza Canyon Petroglyphs

California · Painted

Location: Azuza Canyon, about 30 miles NE of Los Angeles

Painted rock characters

Water connection: Canyon

Sources: W.J. Hoffman; Mallery 1893: pp. 69 (Figs. 31, 467, 468, 469)

Santa Ynez Range Painted Rock

California · Painted (red and black)

Location: 12 miles WNW of Santa Barbara, almost at summit of Santa Ynez Range; gray sandstone rock projecting into narrow mountain canyon

Blanket bale circles with cross-line cords; human figures with black/white banding (Mexican traders); horse carrying bale led by Indian with headdress; centipede; trading expedition scene

Rock: Gray sandstone, 30 ft high, promontory into canyon. Setting: Rounded cavity ~15 ft wide, 8 ft high inside. Attribution: Connected to Indian trails; possibly record of Mexican blanket trading expedition.

Condition: Ceiling between two painting groups disintegrated (~4 ft gap); cave interior larger than entrance

Water connection: Spring 40 yards west; mountain canyon

Sources: W.J. Hoffman (September 1884); Mallery 1893: pp. 69-72 (Figs. 32, 33)

Escondido Mountain Petroglyphs

California · Pecked (not colored); 1/8 to 1/4 inch deep

Location: Summit of mountain 4 miles directly west of Escondido, San Diego County

Nearly obliterated characters; good lookout from eminence

Rock: Two flat granite rocks/bowlders.

Condition: Nearly obliterated

No trails or burials in vicinity

Sources: Willard J. Whitney; Mallery 1893: pp. 62-63

Susanville Area / Willow Creek / Milford

California · Pictographs/petroglyphs

Location: 15 miles south of Susanville on Willow Creek; also at Milford in lower end of valley

No details furnished

Water connection: Creek; valley

Sources: L.L. Frost; Mallery 1893: pp. 69

Porter Creek Petroglyphs (Healdsburg)

California · Petroglyphs

Location: Porter Creek, 1.5 miles SW of Healdsburg; large bowlder of hornblende syenite

Oblong circles/ovals, some containing crosses; similar to Arizona and Nevada types

Rock: Hornblende syenite bowlder.

Water connection: Creek

Sources: Mallery general description; Mallery 1893: pp. 69

San Bernardino Hills Petroglyphs

California · Pictographic records

Location: Hills east of San Bernardino

Resemble Tule River petroglyphs

Sources: Alphonse Pinart; Mallery 1893: pp. 62

Rock Creek Petroglyphs (Rio Del Norte)

Colorado · Pecked

Location: Cliffs on Rock Creek, 15 miles from Rio Del Norte

Men on horseback, cross-shaped human figures, animals; Shoshonean type

Rock: Cliffs. Attribution: Shoshonean type.

Water connection: Creek

Sources: Cyrus F. Newcomb; Mallery 1893: pp. 72

Piedra Pintada Canyon Petroglyphs

Colorado · Carved/cut (not painted; distinct from Ute painted practice)

Location: 20 miles SE of Rio Del Norte, entrance of canyon of Piedra Pintada Creek; north wall of canyon on ranches of W.M. Maguire and V.T. Hudson

All manner of pictures, symbols, hieroglyphics for quarter mile along canyon wall; distinct from softer sandstone carvings of NM/AZ

Rock: Volcanic rocks (hard). Setting: Quarter mile extent along canyon wall. Attribution: Pre-Ute; 'Utes admit them to have been old when their fathers conquered the country'.

Condition: Some of greater antiquity than others but all are ancient; bear marks of age

Water connection: Creek canyon

Utes acknowledge antiquity predating their own occupation

Sources: Capt. E.L. Berthoud; Mallery 1893: pp. 72

Rio Mancos Cliff House Petroglyphs

Colorado · Chipped/engraved; also painted (red and white clay identical

Location: Rio Mancos, near group of cliff houses; CO/NM boundary area

Human figures (arbitrary/symbolic, not naturalistic); cliff-builder work; painting materials match cliff house construction plaster

Rock: Rock faces near cliff houses; massive sandstone blocks. Setting: Approx 1/12 original size in Mallery. Attribution: Cliff builders (Ancestral Puebloan).

Water connection: River

Painted figures probably made during cliff house construction

Sources: W.H. Holmes, Bureau of Ethnology; Brandegee (notes/sketches); Mallery 1893: pp. 73-74 (Figs. 34, 35)

Rio San Juan Petroglyphs

Colorado/New Mexico · Engraved/chipped out (frequently 1/4 to 1/2 inch deep); imme

Location: Rio San Juan, about 10 miles below mouth of Rio La Plata (actually in NM but near CO border)

Great procession of men, birds, beasts, fanciful figures with movement toward right; winged figures hovering; antlered deer drawing notched sledge with two men; figures tied together in continuous line; dogs stationed around procession; possibly migration or victory record; designs resembling pottery embellishment

Rock: Light-colored massive sandstone; smooth-faced blocks from bluff breakdown. Setting: Reduction to ~1/12 original; bluff is low line of light-colored massive sandstone.

Condition: Undoubted evidence of age; new figures easily distinguished by fresh chipped surfaces

Water connection: River bluff

May represent tribal migration or victory trophies

Sources: W.H. Holmes, Bureau of Ethnology; Mallery 1893: pp. 74-75 (Figs. 36, 37)

Scaticook (Cobble Hill) Petroglyph

Connecticut · Pecked (like Dighton Rock); 1/4 to 1 inch wide, 1/10 to 2/10

Location: Township of Kent, place called Scaticook; Cobble Hill, about 100 rods east of Housatonic River

Sides all around irregularly charged with unknown characters; top has no characters

Rock: White flint; isolated rock not part of mountains. Setting: 12-14 ft long, 8-10 ft wide at base and top, uneven surface.

Condition: Appeared very old as of 1789 visit by Dr. Ezra Stiles

Water connection: ~100 rods east of Housatonic River

Sources: Dr. Ezra Stiles (1789); via Rafn's Antiquitates Americanae; Mallery 1893: pp. 75-76

Forsyth County Carved Bowlder

Georgia · Carved/incised (1/2 to 3/4 inch deep)

Location: Forsyth County, Georgia

Circles (plain, nucleated, concentric); some joined by straight lines forming 'spectacle-shaped' figures; cup sculpture type

Rock: Fine-grained granite bowlder. Setting: ~9 ft long, 4 ft 6 inches high, 3 ft broad at widest. Attribution: Generally believed Cherokee.

Sources: Charles C. Jones, Jr.; Mallery 1893: pp. 76 (Figs. 38)

Enchanted Mountain Sculptures

Georgia · Sculptured/cut

Location: Enchanted Mountain, Union County, Georgia

Tracks of men, women, children, deer, bears, bisons, turkeys, terrapins; outlines of snake, two deer, human hand; 'Great Warrior' footprint (18 inches, 6 toes); 136-146 sculptures total

Rock: Plutonic rock. Setting: Great Warrior footprint: 18 inches long with 6 toes.

Condition: Tracks alternate right and left; some left tracks worn by erosion/travel; wagon road passes over rock

Sources: Dr. M.F. Stephenson; Charles C. Jones Jr.; Mallery 1893: pp. 76

Oneida Petroglyphs

Idaho · Petroglyphs; Shoshonean type

Location: Near Oneida, Idaho

Totemic characters; possibly names of visitors; human beings

Rock: Basalt. Attribution: Shoshonean.

Sources: G.K. Gilbert, U.S. Geological Survey; Willard D. Johnson (1879); Mallery 1893: pp. 77 (Figs. 39, 1092)

Piasa Rock

Illinois · Painted (red, black, green)

Location: Immediately above city of Alton, on Mississippi River bluff; near mouth of Piasa Creek

Two monsters 'large as a calf' with deer horns, red eyes, tiger beard, fish tail passing around body over head and between legs; human-like face, body covered with scales; object of Indian worship; called 'The bird which devours men' (Piasa)

Rock: Limestone cliff. Setting: Each figure as large as a calf; 50 ft above base on cliff face.

Condition: Whole face of bluff quarried away 1846-47; described by Marquette 1675; one figure may have been destroyed by rock fracture

Water connection: Mississippi River bluff

Object of Indian worship; Indians discharged rifles and raised shouts when passing; mythic creature Piasa = 'bird which devours men'

Sources: Marquette (1675); Davidson and Strove; McAdams; A.D. Jones (1838); Wm. Dennis (1825 sketch); Mallery 1893: pp. 77-80 (Figs. 40, 41)

Illinois River Petroglyph

Illinois · Sculptured (body carved in outline); archer figure painted (

Location: 25-30 miles above mouth of Illinois River, west bank, high on smooth face of overhanging cliff

Animal-like body with human head; huge saucer-like eye depressions 1+ inch deep; archer with drawn bow painted over sculptured monster face

Rock: Hard rock cliff.

Condition: Probably same condition as when French voyagers first descended; rock sculpture is deeply carved

Water connection: River cliff

Painted figure of human form with bow possibly added later than sculpture

Sources: McAdams; Mallery 1893: pp. 79-80 (Figs. 42)

Alton Cliff Cave Paintings

Illinois · Painted (reddish-brown pigment)

Location: 3-4 miles above Alton, beneath overhanging cliff forming cave shelter, on smooth face of thick rock ledge; 100+ ft above Mississippi River

Series of 12 paintings; stained into rock

Rock: Rock ledge (cave shelter).

Condition: Almost perfectly dry due to sheltered position; appearance denotes great age; changed but little in 30 years of observation

Water connection: Mississippi River cliff; 100+ ft above river

Sources: McAdams; Mallery 1893: pp. 80 (Figs. 43)

Jackson County Mississippi Bluff Petroglyphs

Illinois · Petroglyphs

Location: Bluffs of Mississippi River, Jackson County, about 12 miles below Rockwood

Rock: Bluff.

Water connection: Mississippi River bluffs

Sources: James D. Middleton, Bureau of Ethnology; Mallery 1893: pp. 80

Prairie du Rocher Petroglyphs

Illinois · Petroglyphs

Location: 4-5 miles from Prairie du Rocher, near Mississippi River

Water connection: Near Mississippi River

Sources: James D. Middleton; Mallery 1893: pp. 80

New Albion Cave Petroglyphs

Iowa · Incised (caves); painted (cliffs)

Location: Banks of Mississippi River, NE Iowa, 4 miles south of New Albion; also paintings on cliffs 15 miles south; also painted characters 19 miles below New Albion

Incised petroglyphs in numerous caves; painted characters on cliffs

Rock: Cave walls; cliffs.

Water connection: Mississippi River banks

Sources: P.W. Norris, Bureau of Ethnology; Mallery 1893: pp. 80

Fort Harker Petroglyph

Kansas · Petroglyphs

Location: Near Union Pacific railroad, 15 miles SE of Fort Harker (Fort Ellsworth)

Inscriptions on rock formation

Rock: Lower Cretaceous formation (No. 1, Meek and Hayden classification).

Sources: Edward Miller (Proc. American Philosophical Society, vol. x, 1869); Mallery 1893: pp. 80-81 (Figs. 44)

Union County Petroglyphs (opposite Shawneetown)

Kentucky · Petroglyphs

Location: Union County, nearly opposite Shawneetown, Illinois

Resemble Jackson County Illinois petroglyphs

Water connection: Near Ohio River (opposite Shawneetown)

Sources: James D. Middleton; Mallery 1893: pp. 81

Clover Bottom Track Rock

Kentucky · Sculptured tracks

Location: Clover Bottom, spur of Big Hill, Jackson County, about 13 miles from Berea; near Pine Grove schoolhouse (1/4 mile or less)

Human tracks alternating right and left; bear, horse, dog tracks; larger unidentified tracks; tracks cross strata (proving human manufacture); direction ascending slope

Rock: Sandstone, subcarboniferous; nearly horizontal strata with ~20 degree erosion slope.

Condition: Some left tracks worn out by erosion and travel; so well made 'one is tempted to ask if they can be real'

Rock was covered with soil and vegetation within memory of old settlers; wagon road passes over rock causing exposure

Sources: W.E. Barton (October 1890); Mallery 1893: pp. 81

Bald Friar Rocks / Miles Island

Maryland · Deep sculpting finished by rubbing with stone or wood and sa

Location: Susquehanna River, half mile south of PA state line; 640m south of state line; Miles Island and nearby bowlders; 800m south of French's Tavern

Conventional Indian serpent heads with varying line counts; concentric circles (up to 4); figures appeared connected and of narrative purport; every exposed part covered with figures; some bowlders pictured on undersides

Rock: Dark-greenish chlorite schist; extremely hard. Setting: Main rock surface ~5 ft x 4 ft 6 inches; figures extend beyond horizontal to rounded edge at 45 and 30 degree angles. Attribution: Algonquian type; resembles Owens Valley California and Washington PA petroglyphs.

Condition: Annually subjected to freshets and erosion; becoming more eroded; some displacement by natural agencies and human interference

Water connection: River - rocks in Susquehanna River; near mouth of small stream

Sources: Prof. P. Frazer; Dr. Charles Rau; W.J. Hoffman (1888-1889); Mallery 1893: pp. 83-85 (Figs. 45, 46, 47, 48)

Rutland Stone

Massachusetts · Characters filled with black composition nearly as hard as r

Location: Rutland, Worcester County

Line of considerable length in unknown characters; regularly placed

Rock: Large stone.

Sources: Rafn's Antiq. Amer.; Mallery 1893: pp. 87

Swanzy Rock

Massachusetts

Location: Swanzy, Bristol County, ~10 miles from Dighton Rock

Similar to Dighton Rock

Sources: Rafn's Antiq. Amer.; Mallery 1893: pp. 87

Pipestone Quarry Petroglyphs

Minnesota · Pecked (with sharp-pointed instruments or other quartzite pi

Location: Pipestone Quarry; on glaciated quartzite surface about the 'Three Maidens' bowlders

Totemic characters: bird tracks (crane's foot/snipe most common), pelican outline, deer, turtle, bear, wolf, buffalo, elk, human figures, circle with interior cross; warrior/hunter success records

Rock: Quartzite (extremely hard); also catlinite quarry. Setting: Incisions very slight (~1/16 inch); quartzite preserved them well. Attribution: Multiple tribes; Dakota (Yanktonwanna, Teetonwan); totems of turtle, bear, crane.

Condition: Most stones taken up and removed by relic hunters; original order destroyed

Custom to inscribe totem before quarrying catlinite; sacred ground; lightning legend

Sources: P.W. Norris; R. Gronau; N.H. Winchell; W.H. Holmes (June 1892); J.W. Lynd; Mallery 1893: pp. 87-89 (Figs. 50)

Brown's Valley Bowlder

Minnesota · Incised (neatly done, very slight depth)

Location: Edge of public park, north end of plateau at Brown's Valley; also bowlder 4 miles NW in Roberts County, SD

Man (conventional form), bird, tortoise, cross-and-circle combined, bird tracks, nondescript forms, small dots/cups

Rock: Bowlder with flat surface, western exposure; ~5 ft 8 inches diameter. Setting: Bowlder ~5 ft 8 inches diameter; figures 1/8 natural size in reproduction.

Condition: Firmly imbedded in terrace

Water connection: Minnesota River (high terrace on west side)

Sources: T.H. Lewis; Mallery 1893: pp. 90 (Figs. 51)

Montana · Unknown

Location: Near Fort Assiniboin

Pictured rocks; general type not described

Dakota County Petroglyphs

Nebraska · Carved

Location: Face of sandstone cliff in deep ravine where two watercourses meet; ~20 miles south of Sioux City, Iowa, in Dakota County, NE; narrow cape between two ravines, 50-75 ft high

Rock: Soft sandstone sheltered by overhanging hard jasperous sandstone. Attribution: Possibly Omaha.

Condition: Some figures nearly effaced by time; sheltered position preserved most

Water connection: Near Missouri River bottom; dry watercourses

Cave-like nature made it favorite camping/resting place

Nebraska · Engraved (could be made with piece of wood due to soft stone

Rock: Soft sandstone. Setting: Life-size figures. Attribution: Omaha (term Ingna'ie gikaya-ina = Spirit place).

Nevada · Incised/pecked; very shallow indentation

Rock: Basaltic, ~4 ft high and broad. Setting: Rock ~4 ft high, equally broad. Attribution: Washoe Indians roamed region but know nothing of works.

Condition: Marks plain despite imperceptible depth; moss as hard and dry and old as the stone

North Carolina · Painted (dark red, ferruginous oxide); one black/dark gray f

Rock: Soft sandstone. Setting: Ledge extends 1/8 mile horizontally along hillside. Attribution: Tualati (Atfalati) Indians.

Location: About half mile from head of Lake Chelan, western shore; perpendicular granite cliff rising from water

Water connection: River bay

Sources: T.H. Lewis; Mallery 1893: pp. 127-128 (Figs. 92)

Wind River Valley Petroglyphs

Wyoming · Petroglyphs

Location: Wind River Valley; also Little Popo-Agie

Indian killed another (interpreted by Shoshoni/Bannock 1880); Blackfoot attack on log house: horse with lance, log house by stream, hoof prints, two lance thrusts, bows and arrows captured

Rock: Yellow sandstone (Little Popo-Agie). Attribution: Shoshoni and Bannock interpreted; Blackfoot depicted.

Condition: Some antiquity at Little Popo-Agie

Water connection: River valley

Interpreted by Shoshoni and Bannock delegation to Washington in 1880

Sources: Capt. William A. Jones, U.S. Army (1873); Mallery 1893: pp. 128-130 (Figs. 93, 94, 95, 1091)

Sage Creek Petroglyphs (Fort Washakie)

Wyoming · Petroglyphs

Location: Sandstone rock near headwaters of Sage Creek, near Fort Washakie

Two chief human figures with hands/arms partly uplifted; spirals; Algonquian type; similar to Blackfeet work

Rock: Sandstone. Attribution: Probably Blackfeet (Satsika); Algonquian type.

Water connection: Creek headwaters

Neither Shoshoni nor Arapaho know who made them

Sources: Dr. William H. Corbusier, U.S. Army; Mallery 1893: pp. 129-130 (Figs. 96, 97)

Cara Pintada Petroglyphs

Sonora, Mexico · Petroglyphs

Location: Cara Pintada, 3 miles SW of Huassavas

Very large and interesting

Sources: A. Bandelier (conversation); Mallery 1893: pp. 131

Las Flechas Petroglyphs

Sonora, Mexico · Petroglyphs

Location: Las Flechas, 1 mile west of Huassavas

Smaller than Cara Pintada

Sources: A. Bandelier (conversation); Mallery 1893: pp. 131

Oakley Spring Moki Salt Expedition Petroglyphs

Arizona · Pounded with hard point; some scratched; totemic marks

Location: Oakley Spring, Yavapai County; at base of Vermilion Cliff; fallen blocks of soft sandstone

Each Moki/Oraibi Indian draws his totem (gens symbol) once per salt-gathering visit; rain cloud totem of chief Tubi (last survivor of his gens)

Rock: Homogeneous massive soft sandstone (fallen blocks). Setting: 6 ft long, 4 ft high (one group). Attribution: Moki/Oraibi (Hopi); salt expeditions to Colorado Chiquito canyon.

Condition: Many weather-worn beyond recognition; others so fresh dust not washed away

Water connection: Spring at cliff base

Oraibi chief Tubi explained: Mokis make excursions to Colorado Chiquito for salt, stop at Oakley Spring on return

Sources: G.K. Gilbert, U.S. Geological Survey (1878); Mallery 1893: pp. 329-330 (Figs. 437)
